statement: The rewritten `llm_attempts` ledger and Upstash quota keys should share one normalized vocabulary from the capability registry: keep attempt identity, domain linkage, normalized usage, and payload references as first-class fields, and push provider-specific metadata into `provider_extensions`.
rationale: If the ledger and rate-limit layer derive from different provider vocabularies, the engine will drift back into provider-specific branching. A normalized envelope plus extension payload gives maintainability without erasing provider-specific metadata.
keyAssumptions: Normalized usage fields like `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, and `cached_input_tokens` are enough for most cross-provider analytics and quota accounting
Provider-specific metadata can remain queryable and debuggable inside structured extension payloads
validationCriteria: A new provider can map usage into the generic envelope without changing the top-level attempt schema
Quota keys are generated from normalized dimension IDs instead of provider-specific field names
Provider-specific IDs such as batch/file/request identifiers do not force schema churn in the generic ledger envelope
critiquePoints: If too many important provider details are buried in extensions, analytics and debugging could become cumbersome
Some provider fields may eventually deserve promotion to first-class columns after real usage
questions: Which provider-specific fields should be promoted immediately because they are operationally critical across multiple providers?
